summary: This article presents an alternative to traditional top-down software architecture by introducing a decentralized, conversation-based approach centered on the 'Advice Process.' Rather than having architects make all decisions, anyone can make architectural decisions provided they consult those affected and those with relevant expertise. The approach is supported by four elements: Architectural Decision Records (ADRs), Architecture Advisory Forums, team-sourced principles, and organization-specific technology radars. The author argues this method produces better, faster, more accountable decisions while developing architectural thinking across teams rather than concentrating it in a few individuals.
key_points: The Advice Process allows anyone to make architectural decisions, but requires consulting those affected and those with expertise - seeking input without requiring consensus
Architecture Advisory Forums provide a regular time and place for conversations where advice-seeking happens publicly, enabling organizational learning
Architectural Decision Records serve as both a thinking tool and documentation, capturing context, options, consequences, and all advice received
Team-sourced architectural principles provide direction without control, serving as evaluation criteria that teams feel ownership over
The biggest failure mode is architects failing to trust people and continuing 'shadow architecture' behind the scenes
money_quotes: I stopped taking architectural decisions. Completely.
It is the developer's assumptions which get shipped to production.

It is very much about ensuring that conversations that are needed to be happening are happening - not always initiating them, nor always helping to focus or navigate them, but ensuring they do happen.
We are not looking for consensus here, but we are looking for a broad range of inputs and voices.
The sole benefit of this failure mode is that it becomes evident very rapidly as all the benefits I've listed above fail to materialize.

Once you empower people, giving them an environment in which to succeed, and recognise their successes, they will rapidly, and as a collective, start thinking about things which haven't even crossed your mind.
The real benefit of this kind of approach: access to the collective intelligence of the many, over reliance on the much more restricted intelligence of the few.
If everyone is empowered to make a decision, how do we ensure that the sum of all the individual decisions combine to form a coherent whole?
key_insight: Effective architecture at scale comes not from centralized decision-making but from creating conversational structures that enable distributed decision-making with appropriate advice-seeking, accountability, and shared learning.
